refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. The p95 metric (95th percentile) ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions. Refinement clarifies that this is an end-to-end requirement spanning network, vehicle, and teleoperation subsystems.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure round-trip latency (command transmission + vehicle response + telemetry return) under agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct acceptance testing with tele-operators performing representative tasks. Log p95 latency across test scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The vehicle shall process received commands and execute control actions with latency ≤ 25 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall transmit telemetry (sensor data, status, video) to the tele-operator with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to the Safety Services subsystem.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a low-latency, deterministic teleoperation architecture with edge computing on the vehicle, optimized network protocols (UDP-based), and real-time prioritization of critical control and telemetry streams.
DP-B: Deploy a cloud-based teleoperation relay with regional edge nodes to minimize network hops, implement adaptive bitrate streaming for video, and use predictive buffering.
DP-C: Hybrid approach: vehicle-local control loop with UDP-based command/telemetry, cloud-based monitoring and analytics, and fallback to autonomous safety mode if latency exceeds threshold.
DP-D: Implement a redundant dual-path network architecture (primary + backup) with automatic failover and latency monitoring at each hop.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach combining vehicle-local deterministic control (UDP-based command/telemetry with p95 ≤ 75 ms network + 25 ms vehicle processing + 50 ms telemetry return = 150 ms total), cloud-based monitoring and analytics for non-critical functions, and autonomous safety fallback triggered when latency exceeds 150 ms or network link degrades.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation latency to network infrastructure (must meet agreed conditions), vehicle processing architecture (must support real-time command execution), and safety services (must implement fallback mode).

Decoupling achieved by: (1) isolating critical control loop on vehicle (independent of cloud), (2) using standardized Network Link interface for network abstraction, (3) delegating safety fallback logic to Safety Services module.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids cloud dependency for real-time control (eliminates DP-B latency risk) while retaining cloud benefits for analytics. Fallback mechanism ensures safety even if latency target is missed.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle has sufficient local compute capacity for real-time command processing and sensor fusion.
ASSUMPTION: Network Link interface provides deterministic latency characteristics under agreed conditions (e.g., 4G/5G with <100 ms RTT).
ASSUMPTION: Tele-operator can safely operate vehicle in autonomous fallback mode (e.g., vehicle halts or returns to safe state).
RISK: If vehicle compute is insufficient, real-time processing may not meet 25 ms target, pushing total latency above 150 ms.
RISK: Network conditions may degrade unpredictably; fallback mode must be robust and tested.
RISK: Operator training required to understand and trust autonomous fallback behavior.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) vehicle-side command queue and execution scheduler (real-time OS or deterministic middleware), (2) network protocol selection (e.g., UDP with FEC for reliability without TCP retransmit overhead), (3) telemetry prioritization (critical sensors first, video/analytics second), (4) latency monitoring and fallback trigger logic (owned by Safety Services).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency requirement and coordinates command transmission, telemetry reception, and latency monitoring. It interfaces with Vehicle (for command/telemetry exchange), Network Link (for network abstraction), and Safety Services (for fallback triggering). Teleoperation includes tele-operator interface, command encoding/decoding, telemetry aggregation, and latency instrumentation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from tele-operator console. Latency-critical path.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, video feed) to tele-operator console. Latency-critical path.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying network (4G/5G/WiFi). Teleoperation sends encoded commands and receives encoded telemetry via Network Link. Network Link provides latency metrics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals to Safety Services when latency exceeds threshold or network degrades, triggering autonomous fallback mode.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system; teleoperation is a subsystem. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse ownership and blur responsibility. Not recommended.

Vehicle owns command execution and telemetry generation, but not the tele-operator interface, command encoding, or network abstraction. Latency requirement spans vehicle and network; assigning to Vehicle alone would split responsibility. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for this requirement. It owns tele-operator interface, command/telemetry encoding, network abstraction via Network Link interface, and latency monitoring. Interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert) are clean and well-defined. Highest containment, minimal diffusion.

Safety Services owns fallback logic and safety monitoring, but not the teleoperation control loop itself. Assigning latency requirement to Safety Services would be incorrect; Safety Services reacts to latency violations, not owns them.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of CR-NGHE-008. It encapsulates the tele-operator interface, command/telemetry encoding, network abstraction, and latency instrumentation. The module interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry), Network Link (for network services), and Safety Services (for fallback triggering). Latency monitoring and reporting are internal to Teleoperation; fallback execution is delegated to Safety Services via SafetyAlert.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle processing latency (25 ms target) is not met, Teleoperation cannot achieve overall p95 ≤ 150 ms. Mitigation: Teleoperation must specify and verify Vehicle's command processing SLA via interface contract.

RISK: If Network Link does not provide deterministic latency (e.g., network conditions degrade), Teleoperation's latency target becomes unachievable. Mitigation: Teleoperation must monitor Network Link latency and trigger SafetyAlert if degradation is detected.

RISK: Teleoperation module may become complex if it tries to optimize for all network types (4G, 5G, WiFi, satellite). Mitigation: Delegate network optimization to Network Link abstraction; Teleoperation focuses on command/telemetry encoding and latency monitoring.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle: Teleoperation specifies that Tasking commands must be processed and executed within 25 ms (p95). Clarify the interface contract between Teleoperation and Network Link: Network Link must provide latency metrics (RTT, jitter) and support agreed network conditions. Teleoperation uses these metrics to monitor end-to-end latency and trigger SafetyAlert if threshold is exceeded.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid approach) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 by decomposing the 150 ms end-to-end latency budget into network (75 ms), vehicle processing (25 ms), and telemetry return (50 ms). The hybrid architecture avoids cloud dependency for critical control while retaining cloud benefits. Fallback mechanism ensures safety if latency target is missed. DP-C is feasible, testable, and aligns with safety criticality.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Vehicle (command execution) and Safety Services (fallback triggering). If interfaces are not well-defined, responsibility may diffuse. Mitigation: Enforce strict interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert).

Network Link abstraction is critical; if Network Link does not provide latency metrics or deterministic behavior, Teleoperation cannot monitor and enforce latency SLA. Mitigation: Teleoperation must specify Network Link interface requirements upfront.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment of DP-C. The module owns tele-operator interface, command/telemetry encoding, network abstraction, and latency monitoring. Vehicle and Safety Services are external dependencies with clear interface contracts. Minimal diffusion risk if interfaces are enforced.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-008 is verifiable via latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can instrument command transmission, vehicle processing, and telemetry return to measure end-to-end latency. Acceptance testing with tele-operators performing representative tasks will validate p95 ≤ 150 ms.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns end-to-end latency requirement and coordinates with Vehicle (command execution), Network Link (network services), and Safety Services (fallback). Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) are allocated to Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services respectively. Ownership boundaries are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify vehicle-side command processing architecture (real-time OS, deterministic scheduler) to ensure 25 ms latency target is achievable.

Define interface contracts: (1) Tasking interface specifies command format and processing SLA (25 ms), (2) Telemetry interface specifies sensor data format and transmission SLA (50 ms), (3) Network Link interface specifies latency metrics and network conditions, (4) SafetyAlert interface specifies fallback trigger conditions.
Establish latency monitoring and instrumentation strategy: where are latency measurements taken (tele-operator console, vehicle, network)? How are measurements aggregated and reported?

Define autonomous fallback behavior: what does the vehicle do if latency exceeds 150 ms? (e.g., halt, return to safe state, switch to autonomous mode). This must be specified in Safety Services requirements.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command transmission latency, vehicle processing latency, telemetry return latency, and latency monitoring/fallback). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and allocation to specific modules (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services). The FR-DP-IM mapping is sound and passes all gates, but the requirement is complex enough to warrant decomposition to the next level to ensure each sub-FR is properly designed, allocated, and verified.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR identifies four distinct functional areas: (1) command transmission (Teleoperation -> Vehicle via Network Link), (2) vehicle processing (Vehicle), (3) telemetry return (Vehicle -> Teleoperation via Network Link), and (4) latency monitoring and fallback (Teleoperation + Safety Services). Each area has different design considerations, interfaces, and verification strategies. Decomposing to the next level will clarify ownership, interface contracts, and implementation details.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands to the vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95). [Owned by Teleoperation, interfaces with Vehicle via Tasking, Network Link]
FR-NGHE-008.2: The vehicle shall process received commands and execute control actions with latency ≤ 25 ms (p95). [Owned by Vehicle, interfaces with Teleoperation via Tasking]

FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall transmit telemetry (sensor data, status, video) to the tele-operator with latency ≤ 50 ms (p95). [Owned by Vehicle, interfaces with Teleoperation via Telemetry, Network Link]

FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to the Safety Services subsystem. [Owned by Teleoperation, interfaces with Safety Services via SafetyAlert]

assumptions: Vehicle has sufficient local compute capacity for real-time command processing (25 ms target).
Network Link interface provides deterministic latency characteristics under agreed conditions (e.g., 4G/5G with <100 ms RTT).
Tele-operator can safely operate vehicle in autonomous fallback mode (e.g., vehicle halts or returns to safe state).
Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (e.g., bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Teleoperation module can instrument and measure end-to-end latency with sufficient granularity (per-hop measurement).
open_risks: Vehicle processing latency may exceed 25 ms target if compute architecture is not optimized for real-time execution.
Network conditions may degrade unpredictably; fallback mode must be robust and tested under degraded conditions.
Operator training and trust in autonomous fallback behavior is critical; inadequate training could lead to safety incidents.
Latency monitoring and instrumentation may introduce overhead that increases latency; instrumentation strategy must be carefully designed.
Video streaming (part of telemetry) may consume significant bandwidth and introduce variable latency; adaptive bitrate strategy may be needed.
evidence_needed: Vehicle architecture specification: confirm real-time OS or deterministic middleware is available and can meet 25 ms processing latency.
Network Link interface specification: confirm latency metrics are available and network conditions are as agreed.
Teleoperation latency instrumentation design: specify where measurements are taken, how they are aggregated, and how overhead is minimized.
Autonomous fallback behavior specification: define what vehicle does if latency exceeds 150 ms; ensure safety is maintained.
Acceptance test plan: define test scenarios, network conditions, and success criteria for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency.
Operator training plan: define training content for tele-operators on latency limits, fallback behavior, and safe operation.
